skill.name: media-downloader
skill.description: Search and download images, stock video clips, and YouTube media from natural language requests. Supports trimming videos with ffmpeg and uses optional Pexels, Pixabay, and Unsplash API keys.
skill.summary: Find, download, and trim media assets from stock sources and YouTube.
skill.author: yizhiyanhua-ai

security_audit.summary: Static findings for network access, environment API keys, filesystem writes, and subprocess execution are mostly true positives for the advertised downloader. The subprocess calls avoid shell=True, and documentation shell examples are false positives, but the skill still processes untrusted remote media and writes files locally. No evidence found of prompt injection text or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent.

medium_findings.title: External Media Tools Process User Inputs
medium_findings.description: Confirmed true positive for subprocess usage. The skill invokes yt-dlp, ffprobe, and ffmpeg with user-provided search terms, URLs, and file paths. The calls use argument lists instead of shell=True, which reduces shell injection risk, but local tools still parse untrusted remote media and user paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and tied to user-controlled media operations. Confidence is high for elevated execution risk, but lower than critical because shell expansion is not used and the behavior matches the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Unsanitized Query Text Used in Output Filenames
medium_findings.description: The image and video commands build filenames from args.query by only replacing spaces. Queries containing path separators can influence the path passed to file writes under the chosen output directory, which creates a path traversal and overwrite risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The raw query is visibly used in filenames that are later joined with an output directory and opened for writing. The exploitability depends on chosen query text and existing directories, so medium severity is appropriate.

medium_findings.title: API Keys Used in Third-Party Network Requests
medium_findings.description: The skill reads Pexels, Unsplash, and Pixabay keys from environment variables and sends them to external media APIs. This is expected for the downloader, but it expands secret exposure risk; Pixabay keys are placed in URL query strings that may appear in logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API-key data flow is clear and intentional. Confidence is high for sensitive credential handling, but there is no evidence the keys are sent anywhere other than the advertised media providers.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Are Benign
low_findings.description: Many static external-command hits in README.md, README_CN.md, and SKILL.md are fenced setup or usage examples. They document pip, brew, and media_cli.py commands and are not executed by the skill files themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation text and code fences, not executable program logic. The same examples are consistent with the advertised install and usage flow.

low_findings.title: Local Tool Checks Reveal Limited System State
low_findings.description: The status and initialization paths check whether yt-dlp, ffmpeg, and requests are installed. This is mild local reconnaissance, but it only reports dependency availability needed for the skill to function.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code checks a small fixed set of expected dependencies. No broad directory, process, or host enumeration was found in the reviewed locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Entropy Flags Appear False Positive
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography and high-entropy alerts appear to be triggered by words such as media, markdown content, Chinese text, and emoji-rich documentation. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, packed payloads, or obfuscated executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines did not show cryptographic operations or obfuscation. The files are readable documentation and Python source.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Invokes Download and Transcoding Tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The code invokes yt-dlp, ffprobe, and ffmpeg. These calls are legitimate for media downloading and trimming, but they run external binaries against user-provided or downloaded media.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external tool invocations are explicit and confirmed. The absence of shell=True reduces injection risk but does not remove binary parser risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP Requests to External Media Providers
dangerous_patterns.description: The code makes network requests to Pexels, Unsplash, Pixabay, and media URLs returned by those services. This is core functionality and should be disclosed to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs and session calls directly show outbound network behavior. The destinations match the marketplace description.

dangerous_patterns.title: Output Paths Derived from User Inputs
dangerous_patterns.description: The code allows custom output directories and also derives filenames from raw search query text. This can write files outside the intended downloads directory if unsafe path text is used.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: File write locations and filename construction are visible in the code. The risk is practical but bounded by user invocation and local filesystem permissions.

content.value_statement: Finding usable images and clips across media sources takes time and manual cleanup. This skill searches approved providers, downloads assets, and trims videos from one workflow.
actual_capabilities: Searches Pexels, Pixabay, and Unsplash for image results when API keys are configured.
Downloads stock images and stock video clips to a local downloads folder or chosen output folder.
Searches YouTube through yt-dlp and can download videos or audio.
Trims video files with ffmpeg using start, end, or target duration options.
Reports configuration status for API keys, requests, yt-dlp, and ffmpeg.
Supports English and Chinese usage examples and command descriptions.
limitations: Image and stock video search require third-party API keys.
YouTube download behavior depends on yt-dlp support and source availability.
Video trimming requires ffmpeg and may re-encode media files.
Users must verify license terms for each downloaded asset before publication.

use_cases.title: Collect Concept Images
use_cases.description: Find and download reference images for a design board or creative brief.

use_cases.title: Prepare Short Background Clips
use_cases.description: Download stock clips and trim them to a fixed duration for presentations or social posts.

use_cases.title: Extract Review Segments
use_cases.description: Download a specific segment from a YouTube video for private review or editing notes.

prompt_templates.title: Download Basic Images
prompt_templates.prompt: Download 5 landscape images and save them in the default media downloader folder.

prompt_templates.title: Find Short Stock Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Find 3 ocean wave video clips under 20 seconds and download them for review.

prompt_templates.title: Trim a YouTube Segment
prompt_templates.prompt: Download this YouTube video from 90 seconds to 120 seconds and save only that clip.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Media Pack
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a project media pack with 8 coffee shop photos and 2 short ambience clips under 15 seconds.

output_examples.output: The skill searches configured image providers, downloads matching photos, and reports the saved folder path.

output_examples.input: Download a 30 second city night video.
output_examples.output: The skill finds stock video results, downloads a matching clip, trims it if needed, and lists the final file location.
The skill reports whether API keys, requests, yt-dlp, and ffmpeg are available.

best_practices: Set API keys only in trusted local environment variables.
Use a dedicated output folder for each project.
Review provider licenses before publishing downloaded media.
anti_patterns: Do not download media from URLs you do not trust.
Do not use path separators or shell syntax in search queries.
Do not assume every YouTube or stock result is licensed for your use.

faq.question: Does this skill need API keys?
faq.answer: Yes for stock image and video search. YouTube download uses yt-dlp and does not need those API keys.

faq.question: Where are downloads saved?
faq.answer: By default, files are saved in the skill downloads folder. Users can choose another output directory.

faq.question: Can it trim videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses ffmpeg to trim by start time, end time, or target duration.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish downloaded assets?
faq.answer: You must verify the source license and usage rights for each asset before publishing.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It uses network requests, API keys, local file writes, and external media tools. These are expected but sensitive operations.

faq.question: Does it send my API keys to unknown servers?
faq.answer: No evidence found of unknown exfiltration. Keys are used for the advertised Pexels, Pixabay, and Unsplash requests.
